  1. Part 4: Visualization
  2. Static Visualization with Plotly

Static Visualization with Plotly

Lesson 13

Learning Objectives

After this lesson, you will be able to:

  • Create figure objects and traces in Plotly
  • Build line charts, bar charts, and distribution plots
  • Use subplots and faceting for multi-panel displays
  • Apply colorblind-friendly palettes
  • Customize axes, legends, and annotations

Visualizing for Leadership

The audience for your visualizations determines their design. In higher education financial analysis, you’ll typically present to three very different audiences:

  • Executive leadership (Provost, Chancellor, Board of Regents): They want the headline finding, the trend, and the implication. Use clean, uncluttered charts with clear titles that state the takeaway (“Faculty Salaries Have Declined 8% in Real Terms Since 2015”). Limit to 2-3 key data series. Always include a benchmark or reference line (inflation, peer median, policy target).
  • Department chairs and deans: They want to see their unit’s data in context. Comparative bar charts, rank-ordered lists, and peer group distributions work well. They’ll ask “where do I stand?” — make that easy to answer at a glance.
  • Technical analysts and HR partners: They can handle density, detail, and interactivity. Distribution plots, scatter matrices, and drill-down dashboards serve this audience well.

A common mistake is building analyst-grade visualizations for executive audiences. A chart with 15 overlapping time series and no narrative title will lose a Provost in seconds. The best financial visualizations tell a story — they show what happened, why it matters, and what should be done about it.

Introduction

Plotly creates interactive visualizations that work well in Quarto documents. Unlike matplotlib, Plotly charts have built-in zoom, pan, and hover features.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Sample salary trend data
dates = pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS")
trends = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": list(dates) * 3,
    "category": ["Faculty"] * 8 + ["Academic Staff"] * 8 + ["University Staff"] * 8,
    "median_salary": (
        [95000, 97000, 99000, 101000, 103000, 105000, 107000, 110000] +
        [72000, 73500, 75000, 76500, 78000, 79500, 81000, 83000] +
        [55000, 56000, 57000, 58000, 59000, 60000, 61000, 62500]
    )
})

Plotly Express: Quick Charts

Plotly Express provides high-level functions for common chart types:

Line Charts

fig = px.line(
    trends,
    x="date",
    y="median_salary",
    color="category",
    title="Median Salary Trends by Employee Category",
    labels={"median_salary": "Median Salary ($)", "date": "Year", "category": "Category"}
)

fig.update_layout(
    legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=-0.3),
    hovermode="x unified"
)

fig.show()
TipIn R 
library(plotly)
library(ggplot2)

# ggplot2 approach
p <- ggplot(trends, aes(x = date, y = median_salary, color = category)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(title = "Median Salary Trends")

ggplotly(p)

Bar Charts

# Latest salary by category
latest = trends[trends["date"] == trends["date"].max()]

fig = px.bar(
    latest,
    x="category",
    y="median_salary",
    color="category",
    title="Current Median Salary by Category"
)

fig.update_layout(showlegend=False)
fig.show()

Distribution Plots

# Generate salary distribution data
np.random.seed(42)
salary_dist = pd.DataFrame({
    "salary": np.concatenate([
        np.random.normal(100000, 15000, 100),
        np.random.normal(75000, 12000, 150),
        np.random.normal(58000, 8000, 120)
    ]),
    "category": ["Faculty"] * 100 + ["Academic Staff"] * 150 + ["University Staff"] * 120
})

fig = px.box(
    salary_dist,
    x="category",
    y="salary",
    color="category",
    title="Salary Distribution by Category"
)
fig.show()

Graph Objects: More Control

For complex visualizations, use graph_objects:

fig = go.Figure()

# Add traces for each category
for cat in trends["category"].unique():
    cat_data = trends[trends["category"] == cat]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=cat_data["date"],
        y=cat_data["median_salary"],
        mode="lines+markers",
        name=cat,
        hovertemplate="%{y:$,.0f}<extra></extra>"
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Salary Trends (Graph Objects)",
    xaxis_title="Date",
    yaxis_title="Median Salary",
    yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f"
)

fig.show()

Subplots

Create multi-panel displays:

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=("Nominal Salary", "Salary Growth Rate")
)

# Panel 1: Nominal salary
for cat in ["Faculty", "Academic Staff"]:
    cat_data = trends[trends["category"] == cat]
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=cat_data["date"], y=cat_data["median_salary"], name=cat, mode="lines"),
        row=1, col=1
    )

# Panel 2: Growth rate (simulated)
growth_data = trends.copy()
growth_data["growth"] = growth_data.groupby("category")["median_salary"].pct_change() * 100

for cat in ["Faculty", "Academic Staff"]:
    cat_data = growth_data[growth_data["category"] == cat].dropna()
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=cat_data["date"], y=cat_data["growth"], name=cat, mode="lines", showlegend=False),
        row=1, col=2
    )

fig.update_layout(height=400, title_text="Salary Analysis")
fig.show()

Colorblind-Friendly Palettes

# Wong colorblind-friendly palette
wong_palette = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442", "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7"]

fig = px.line(
    trends,
    x="date",
    y="median_salary",
    color="category",
    color_discrete_sequence=wong_palette,
    title="Using Colorblind-Friendly Palette"
)
fig.show()

Customization

fig = px.line(trends, x="date", y="median_salary", color="category")

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text="Customized Chart", x=0.5, font=dict(size=20)),
    xaxis=dict(title="Year", showgrid=True, gridcolor="lightgray"),
    yaxis=dict(title="Salary ($)", tickformat="$,.0f", showgrid=True),
    legend=dict(title="Category", orientation="h", y=-0.2),
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    font=dict(family="Arial", size=12)
)

fig.show()

Exercise

Exercise 13: Salary Visualization Suite

  1. Create a line chart showing salary trends over time
  2. Create a bar chart comparing current salaries across divisions
  3. Create a box plot showing salary distributions
  4. Apply a colorblind-friendly palette
  5. Create a two-panel subplot comparing nominal vs real salary
NoteSolution 
import plotly.express as px
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Sample data
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering"]
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "division": divisions * 2,
    "year": [2022] * 3 + [2024] * 3,
    "salary": [85000, 105000, 95000, 90000, 112000, 102000],
    "real_salary": [85000, 105000, 95000, 82000, 102000, 93000]
})

# 1. Line chart
fig1 = px.line(df, x="year", y="salary", color="division", title="1. Salary Trends")
fig1.show()

# 2. Bar chart (2024)
current = df[df["year"] == 2024]
fig2 = px.bar(current, x="division", y="salary", title="2. Current Salaries by Division")
fig2.show()

# 3. Box plot
np.random.seed(42)
box_data = pd.DataFrame({
    "salary": np.concatenate([np.random.normal(m, 10000, 30) for m in [85000, 105000, 95000]]),
    "division": ["L&S"] * 30 + ["Medicine"] * 30 + ["Engineering"] * 30
})
fig3 = px.box(box_data, x="division", y="salary", title="3. Salary Distributions")
fig3.show()

# 4. Colorblind palette (already applied)
wong = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73"]
fig4 = px.bar(current, x="division", y="salary", color="division",
              color_discrete_sequence=wong, title="4. Colorblind-Friendly")
fig4.show()

# 5. Subplot
fig5 = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=("Nominal", "Real (2021$)"))
for i, div in enumerate(divisions):
    d = df[df["division"] == div]
    fig5.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["year"], y=d["salary"], name=div), row=1, col=1)
    fig5.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["year"], y=d["real_salary"], name=div, showlegend=False), row=1, col=2)
fig5.update_layout(title="5. Nominal vs Real Salary")
fig5.show()

Summary

You’ve learned Plotly fundamentals:

  • Plotly Express: Quick charts with px.line(), px.bar(), px.box()
  • Graph Objects: Fine-grained control with go.Figure() and go.Scatter()
  • Subplots: Multi-panel displays with make_subplots()
  • Customization: Layouts, colors, axes, legends

Next Steps

Continue to Lesson 14: Interactive Dashboards in Quarto.

Source Code
---
title: "Static Visualization with Plotly"
subtitle: "Lesson 13"
---

::: {.learning-objectives}
#### Learning Objectives

After this lesson, you will be able to:

- Create figure objects and traces in Plotly
- Build line charts, bar charts, and distribution plots
- Use subplots and faceting for multi-panel displays
- Apply colorblind-friendly palettes
- Customize axes, legends, and annotations
:::

::: {.concept-box}
#### Visualizing for Leadership

The audience for your visualizations determines their design. In higher education financial analysis, you'll typically present to three very different audiences:

- **Executive leadership (Provost, Chancellor, Board of Regents)**: They want the headline finding, the trend, and the implication. Use clean, uncluttered charts with clear titles that state the takeaway ("Faculty Salaries Have Declined 8% in Real Terms Since 2015"). Limit to 2-3 key data series. Always include a benchmark or reference line (inflation, peer median, policy target).
- **Department chairs and deans**: They want to see their unit's data in context. Comparative bar charts, rank-ordered lists, and peer group distributions work well. They'll ask "where do I stand?" — make that easy to answer at a glance.
- **Technical analysts and HR partners**: They can handle density, detail, and interactivity. Distribution plots, scatter matrices, and drill-down dashboards serve this audience well.

A common mistake is building analyst-grade visualizations for executive audiences. A chart with 15 overlapping time series and no narrative title will lose a Provost in seconds. **The best financial visualizations tell a story** — they show what happened, why it matters, and what should be done about it.
:::

## Introduction

Plotly creates interactive visualizations that work well in Quarto documents. Unlike matplotlib, Plotly charts have built-in zoom, pan, and hover features.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

# Sample salary trend data
dates = pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS")
trends = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": list(dates) * 3,
    "category": ["Faculty"] * 8 + ["Academic Staff"] * 8 + ["University Staff"] * 8,
    "median_salary": (
        [95000, 97000, 99000, 101000, 103000, 105000, 107000, 110000] +
        [72000, 73500, 75000, 76500, 78000, 79500, 81000, 83000] +
        [55000, 56000, 57000, 58000, 59000, 60000, 61000, 62500]
    )
})
```

## Plotly Express: Quick Charts

Plotly Express provides high-level functions for common chart types:

### Line Charts

```{python}
fig = px.line(
    trends,
    x="date",
    y="median_salary",
    color="category",
    title="Median Salary Trends by Employee Category",
    labels={"median_salary": "Median Salary ($)", "date": "Year", "category": "Category"}
)

fig.update_layout(
    legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=-0.3),
    hovermode="x unified"
)

fig.show()
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R

```r
library(plotly)
library(ggplot2)

# ggplot2 approach
p <- ggplot(trends, aes(x = date, y = median_salary, color = category)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(title = "Median Salary Trends")

ggplotly(p)
```
:::

### Bar Charts

```{python}
# Latest salary by category
latest = trends[trends["date"] == trends["date"].max()]

fig = px.bar(
    latest,
    x="category",
    y="median_salary",
    color="category",
    title="Current Median Salary by Category"
)

fig.update_layout(showlegend=False)
fig.show()
```

### Distribution Plots

```{python}
# Generate salary distribution data
np.random.seed(42)
salary_dist = pd.DataFrame({
    "salary": np.concatenate([
        np.random.normal(100000, 15000, 100),
        np.random.normal(75000, 12000, 150),
        np.random.normal(58000, 8000, 120)
    ]),
    "category": ["Faculty"] * 100 + ["Academic Staff"] * 150 + ["University Staff"] * 120
})

fig = px.box(
    salary_dist,
    x="category",
    y="salary",
    color="category",
    title="Salary Distribution by Category"
)
fig.show()
```

## Graph Objects: More Control

For complex visualizations, use `graph_objects`:

```{python}
fig = go.Figure()

# Add traces for each category
for cat in trends["category"].unique():
    cat_data = trends[trends["category"] == cat]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=cat_data["date"],
        y=cat_data["median_salary"],
        mode="lines+markers",
        name=cat,
        hovertemplate="%{y:$,.0f}<extra></extra>"
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    title="Salary Trends (Graph Objects)",
    xaxis_title="Date",
    yaxis_title="Median Salary",
    yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f"
)

fig.show()
```

## Subplots

Create multi-panel displays:

```{python}
fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=("Nominal Salary", "Salary Growth Rate")
)

# Panel 1: Nominal salary
for cat in ["Faculty", "Academic Staff"]:
    cat_data = trends[trends["category"] == cat]
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=cat_data["date"], y=cat_data["median_salary"], name=cat, mode="lines"),
        row=1, col=1
    )

# Panel 2: Growth rate (simulated)
growth_data = trends.copy()
growth_data["growth"] = growth_data.groupby("category")["median_salary"].pct_change() * 100

for cat in ["Faculty", "Academic Staff"]:
    cat_data = growth_data[growth_data["category"] == cat].dropna()
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=cat_data["date"], y=cat_data["growth"], name=cat, mode="lines", showlegend=False),
        row=1, col=2
    )

fig.update_layout(height=400, title_text="Salary Analysis")
fig.show()
```

## Colorblind-Friendly Palettes

```{python}
# Wong colorblind-friendly palette
wong_palette = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442", "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7"]

fig = px.line(
    trends,
    x="date",
    y="median_salary",
    color="category",
    color_discrete_sequence=wong_palette,
    title="Using Colorblind-Friendly Palette"
)
fig.show()
```

## Customization

```{python}
fig = px.line(trends, x="date", y="median_salary", color="category")

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text="Customized Chart", x=0.5, font=dict(size=20)),
    xaxis=dict(title="Year", showgrid=True, gridcolor="lightgray"),
    yaxis=dict(title="Salary ($)", tickformat="$,.0f", showgrid=True),
    legend=dict(title="Category", orientation="h", y=-0.2),
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    font=dict(family="Arial", size=12)
)

fig.show()
```

## Exercise {#exercise}

::: {.exercise-box}
#### Exercise 13: Salary Visualization Suite

1. Create a line chart showing salary trends over time
2. Create a bar chart comparing current salaries across divisions
3. Create a box plot showing salary distributions
4. Apply a colorblind-friendly palette
5. Create a two-panel subplot comparing nominal vs real salary

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution

```{python}
import plotly.express as px
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# Sample data
divisions = ["L&S", "Medicine", "Engineering"]
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "division": divisions * 2,
    "year": [2022] * 3 + [2024] * 3,
    "salary": [85000, 105000, 95000, 90000, 112000, 102000],
    "real_salary": [85000, 105000, 95000, 82000, 102000, 93000]
})

# 1. Line chart
fig1 = px.line(df, x="year", y="salary", color="division", title="1. Salary Trends")
fig1.show()

# 2. Bar chart (2024)
current = df[df["year"] == 2024]
fig2 = px.bar(current, x="division", y="salary", title="2. Current Salaries by Division")
fig2.show()

# 3. Box plot
np.random.seed(42)
box_data = pd.DataFrame({
    "salary": np.concatenate([np.random.normal(m, 10000, 30) for m in [85000, 105000, 95000]]),
    "division": ["L&S"] * 30 + ["Medicine"] * 30 + ["Engineering"] * 30
})
fig3 = px.box(box_data, x="division", y="salary", title="3. Salary Distributions")
fig3.show()

# 4. Colorblind palette (already applied)
wong = ["#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73"]
fig4 = px.bar(current, x="division", y="salary", color="division",
              color_discrete_sequence=wong, title="4. Colorblind-Friendly")
fig4.show()

# 5. Subplot
fig5 = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=("Nominal", "Real (2021$)"))
for i, div in enumerate(divisions):
    d = df[df["division"] == div]
    fig5.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["year"], y=d["salary"], name=div), row=1, col=1)
    fig5.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d["year"], y=d["real_salary"], name=div, showlegend=False), row=1, col=2)
fig5.update_layout(title="5. Nominal vs Real Salary")
fig5.show()
```
:::
:::

## Summary

You've learned Plotly fundamentals:

- **Plotly Express**: Quick charts with `px.line()`, `px.bar()`, `px.box()`
- **Graph Objects**: Fine-grained control with `go.Figure()` and `go.Scatter()`
- **Subplots**: Multi-panel displays with `make_subplots()`
- **Customization**: Layouts, colors, axes, legends

## Next Steps

Continue to [Lesson 14: Interactive Dashboards in Quarto](14-interactive-dashboards.qmd).
 

Built with Quarto | Data: UW-Madison Public Salary Records

  • Edit this page
  • Report an issue
